mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M5 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW M5 engine typically costs £5,000–£14,000+ supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the S62 at £5,000–£8,000 to the S85 at £8,000–£14,000+ and the S63 at £7,000–£12,000+. The S68 PHEV in the G90 costs £10,000–£16,000+. Fitting adds £650–£2,100. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M5 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S62B50 in the E39 — one of BMW's most reliable M engines with no catastrophic failure modes. The S63B44 in the F90 is proven and robust. The S85B50 in the E60 is a masterpiece but carries rod bearing and SMG pump caveats.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M5 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an S85 (E60) without documented rod bearing replacement or SMG pump history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine is a masterpiece; the known risks are the problem. An F90 S63 has no such caveats.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my M5 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: rod bearing knock (S85), metallic noise at high RPM (S85), oil drips (S63), SMG pump failure (E60), VANOS rattling (S38/S62). On the S85, cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is a warning. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £5,000–£14,000+ engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M5 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £50,000 F90, an £8,000–£12,000+ S63 recon is 16–24% — ✅ safe. Against a £100,000+ G90, a £10,000–£16,000+ S68 recon is 10–16% — ✅ safe. Against a £20,000 E39, a £5,000–£8,000 S62 recon is 25–40% — ✅ safe (the E39 is a keeper). Against a £15,000 E60, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 53–93% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M5 should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: F90 M5 Competition with the S63 — 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£50,000–£70,000). For the classic, E39 M5 with the S62 — the greatest M5 ever made, no caveats, and a modern classic. For the future, G90 with the S68 PHEV — the most powerful M5 ever made, and the Touring is back. For the V10 experience, E60 with the S85 — the only V10 M5, but budget for rod bearings (£3,000–£5,000) before you buy.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M5 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E28, E34, E39, E60, F10, F90, G90) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW M5 vs M3 — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Mechanically they share some engines — the S63 in the F90 M5 is a V8 while the M3 uses the S58 straight-six. The S58 is widely regarded as more reliable than the S63, but both are proven. The M3 is lighter and works the powertrain less hard; the M5 is heavier but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The M3 offers more engagement; the M5 offers more luxury and power.

hero.h1: M5 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW M5 engine across seven generations — from the M88/3 straight-six of the E28 to the S68 plug-in hybrid V8 of the G90. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's ultimate super saloon. Backed by real UK M5 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The M5 rule — power has a price: an S63 recon at £8,000–£12,000+ against a £40,000 F90 is 20–30% — ✅ safe. Against a £100,000+ G90, an S68 recon is 10–15% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 E60, an S85 recon at £8,000–£14,000+ is 53–93% — ⚠️ watch. Generation decides everything.
★★★★☆ — the S62 and S63 are proven performers; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S68 in the G90 is too new for volume data but BMW's latest V8 is promising
S63B44 (F90) — the twin-turbo V8 is proven and robust
S85B50 (E60/E61) — rod bearing failure, SMG pump failure, VANOS issues
F90 M5 Competition with S63 — 625 bhp, xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory
G90 M5 with S68 PHEV — 727 bhp, the most powerful M5 ever, and the return of the Touring
S85 rod bearings / S63 oil leaks — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
👑 The Super Saloon — BMW's ultimate performance sedan and the car that defined the segment

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The M5 is BMW's ultimate super saloon — seven generations of engineering excellence and brutal performance. The S62 and S63 are proven performers; the S85 V10 is a masterpiece with rod bearing caveats; the S68 in the G90 is the most powerful M5 ever. Buy the newest you can afford, and on any E60 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy.

rows.value: S85B50 (E60) — rod bearing failure, SMG pump failure
H2: BMW M5 Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Seven generations, 40 years, and one nameplate that defined the super saloon segment. The right M5 depends on your budget, your engine preference, and your tolerance for maintenance.

rows.value: Our Verdict: The original M5 — hand-built and the fastest production saloon of its era. The M88/3 engine was derived from the BMW M1 supercar, revving to 7,000 rpm with individual throttle bodies. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The E28 M5 is the ultimate M5 investment.

Our Verdict: The second-generation M5 — available as a saloon and, for the first time, a Touring estate. The S38 straight-six is a masterpiece of 1990s engineering, with individual throttle bodies and a 7,000 rpm redline. Cooling systems and VANOS are the defining caveats. A rising classic.

rows.metric: BMW M5 E39 🔥 Most Enquired — classic era [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The M5 at its peak — often called the greatest M5 ever made. The S62 was BMW's first V8 M engine, producing 400 bhp with individual throttle bodies and Double VANOS. The E39 M5 is a modern classic — buy the best you can afford.

rows.metric: BMW M5 E60/E61 🔥 Most Enquired — S85 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The only V10 M5 — the S85 was inspired by BMW's Formula One program, revving to 8,250 rpm. The rod bearing failure is the defining risk: budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement. The SMG III gearbox is also a weak point. A masterpiece — but a costly one.

Our Verdict: The first turbocharged M5 — and the last rear-wheel-drive M5. The S63 twin-turbo V8 produces 560 bhp (standard) to 600 bhp (30th Anniversary). The engine is proven but carries oil leak and turbo issues. The F10 is the M5 for the modern era — powerful, refined, and depreciated.

rows.metric: BMW M5 F90 🔥 Most Enquired — S63 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The first all-wheel-drive M5 — and the most capable. The S63 produces 600 bhp (standard), 625 bhp (Competition), and 635 bhp (CS). The M xDrive system can be deactivated for pure rear-wheel-drive fun. The F90 is the M5 for the modern era — buy it with confidence.
2024–present • S68B44 4.4L twin-turbo V8 PHEV • 727 bhp / 738 lb-ft

Our Verdict: The seventh-generation M5 — and the first plug-in hybrid. The S68 combines a 585 PS 4.4L V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. Available as a saloon (G90) and a Touring (G99). Too new for volume reliability data, but the S63 lineage is proven.
The Engine Choice — The Line That Matters Most
The V10 masterpiece — budget for bearings
Read the full comparison: BMW M5 F10 vs F90 →

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "M5" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "M5" badge covers six different engines across seven generations — the M88/3/S38 straight-six (E28/E34), S62 V8 (E39), S85 V10 (E60), S63 twin-turbo V8 (F10/F90), and S68 PHEV V8 (G90). All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. The engine family — not the badge — determines ownership experience and costs.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M5 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW M5 — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: S63B44 and S62B50 ratings: 90% confidence — well-documented across the M range with clear patterns from specialist rebuilders. S85B50 rating: 85% confidence — the V10 is well-documented with rod bearing and SMG pump failures. S68B44 rating: Provisional — the G90 is too new for volume data; ratings reflect early patterns and shared-architecture evidence from the S63. E28/E34 engines: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns from the classic M5 era. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The S85 V10 (E60) rod bearing failure is the defining issue of the generation — bearings fail catastrophically, taking the crankshaft with them. Preventative replacement is recommended at 60,000–80,000 miles. Correction applied: The S63 (F10/F90) is based on the N63 V8 architecture and carries oil leak issues (valve cover gaskets, turbo oil lines) and turbocharger problems. The F90's S63 is more refined than the F10's. Correction applied: The S62 (E39) is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes, just standard maintenance. Correction applied: The G90/G99 M5's S68 is a plug-in hybrid — combining a 585 PS 4.4L V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for a combined 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. This is the first electrified M5.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the M5 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining E60 M5 issue — rod bearings fail catastrophically, scoring the crankshaft and destroying the engine. Cold-start knock or metallic noise at high RPM is the warning. Affects E60/E61 M5 (2004–2010). £8,000–£14,000+ recon. Preventative replacement: £3,000–£5,000.
The SMG III automated manual gearbox pump fails, leaving the car stuck in gear. Affects E60/E61 M5 (2004–2010). £2,500–£5,000 repair. Many owners convert to manual.
The S63 twin-turbo V8 suffers from oil leaks — valve cover gaskets, oil filter housing, and turbo oil lines. Affects F10 and F90 M5. £1,500–£3,000 repair if caught early; engine damage if ignored.
The S63's turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation or heat soak; high-pressure fuel pumps can also fail. Affects F10 and F90 M5. Turbo replacement: £3,000–£6,000; HPFP: £1,500–£3,000.
The E28 and E34 M5's cooling systems are common failure points — water pumps, radiators, and thermostat housings degrade. Overheating kills the alloy heads. Replace proactively on any classic M5.

The E39 M5's S62 is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes. Just standard maintenance: VANOS solenoids, cooling system, and suspension components. The E39 is the M5 to buy for worry-free classic ownership.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW M5 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the F10 is a modern classic [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the F90 is the most capable M5 [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the V10 M5 is appreciating [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The M5 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (S85 rod bearings, S63 oil leaks) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The M5 buyer knows what they're getting into — they just want to know which generation and which engine to choose. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The F90 M5 Competition is the sweet spot in the used market — 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven S63 engine, and depreciated into sensible territory (£50,000–£70,000). The S63 is robust; the xDrive system makes it devastatingly fast. The F90 is the M5 for buyers who want the ultimate modern super saloon. (Editorial synthesis.)

The E60 M5 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating classic — 8,250 rpm, 507 bhp, and the only V10 M5 ever made. Rod bearings are the defining risk — budget £3,000–£5,000 for preventative replacement or buy one that's already been done. The SMG III pump is the other caveat — budget £2,500–£5,000 for that too. The E60 is a masterpiece — but a costly one. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW M5 Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one M5 based on this page, buy an F90 M5 Competition with the S63 — it's the sweet spot in the range: 625 bhp, M xDrive, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory. If your budget stretches, the G90 with the S68 PHEV is the most powerful M5 ever made — and the Touring is back. And on an E60 S85, budget for rod bearings before you buy — it's £3,000–£5,000 well spent.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW M5 Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW M5 engine typically takes 16–26 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The M5 rule — power has a price: An £8,000–£12,000+ S63 recon against a £50,000 F90 sits at 16–24% — ✅ safe. Against a £100,000+ G90, a £10,000–£16,000+ S68 recon is 10–16% — ✅ safe. Against a £15,000 E60, an £8,000–£14,000+ S85 recon is 53–93% — ⚠️ watch. Against a £20,000 E39, a £5,000–£8,000 S62 recon is 25–40% — ✅ safe (the E39 is a keeper). Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Factor in preventative work (S85 rod bearings: £3,000–£5,000; S63 oil leaks: £1,500–£3,000) as part of the purchase cost — it's the smart money on any M5.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW M5 Engine Range Evolved — 1984 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW created the M5 by fitting the M1's engine into the E28 5 Series. The M88/3 was a 3.5L straight-six derived from the M1 supercar, producing 286 bhp.
eras.worthKnowing: The E28 M5 was hand-built — the fastest production saloon of its era. Now a genuine classic where engine work is restoration, not repair.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The second-generation M5 refined the formula — the S38 was a development of the M88 with improved emissions and power (315–340 bhp). The Touring estate arrived in 1992.
eras.worthKnowing: The S38 straight-six is a masterpiece of 1990s engineering — individual throttle bodies, 7,000 rpm redline, and a rising classic.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's first V8 M engine — the S62 was a 4.9L naturally-aspirated V8 with individual throttle bodies and Double VANOS, producing 400 bhp.
eras.worthKnowing: The S62 is one of BMW's most reliable M engines — no catastrophic failure modes. The E39 M5 is a modern classic — buy the best you can afford.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's only V10 M engine — inspired by Formula One, revving to 8,250 rpm and producing 507 bhp. The SMG III gearbox was a new innovation.
eras.worthKnowing: The S85 is a masterpiece — but rod bearings and SMG pumps are the defining risks. Budget £3,000–£5,000 for rod bearings before you buy.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The first turbocharged M5 — the S63 was a 4.4L twin-turbo V8 producing 560–600 bhp. The last rear-wheel-drive M5.
eras.worthKnowing: The S63 is proven but carries oil leak and turbo issues. The F10 is the M5 for buyers who want the last RWD M5.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The first all-wheel-drive M5 — M xDrive could be deactivated for RWD fun. The S63 produced 600–635 bhp. The F90 is the most capable M5 ever made.
eras.worthKnowing: The F90 M5 Competition is the sweet spot — 625 bhp, proven engine, and depreciated into sensible territory.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The first plug-in hybrid M5 — combining a 585 PS V8 with a 197 PS electric motor for 727 PS and 1,000 Nm of torque. The Touring (G99) is back.
eras.worthKnowing: The G90 is the most powerful M5 ever — and the heaviest. Too new for volume reliability data, but the S63 lineage is proven.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW M5?

profiles.verdict: The M5 is built for this — 400–727 bhp, super saloon performance, and one of the finest chassis in the world. The F90 Competition is the used sweet spot (£50,000–£70,000); the G90 is the future. Buy the newest S63 you can afford, or hunt for a well-maintained F90 Competition.
The E39 M5 is a modern classic — the greatest M5 ever made. The E60 M5 with the S85 V10 is the appreciating masterpiece — 8,250 rpm and the only V10 M5. Both are worth proper investment.
The M5 is a daily-drivable super saloon — the F90 is surprisingly comfortable and practical; the G90 is more refined. The F90 is the better daily driver; the E60 is a weekend car.
The M5 is a heavy super saloon — not as track-focused as the M3 or M4, but the F90's xDrive makes it devastatingly fast. Budget for cooling upgrades and brake pads.

The E39 M5 is the ultimate keeper — the greatest M5 ever made and a modern classic. The F90 M5 Competition is the modern keeper — the most capable M5 ever and the last pure V8 M5 before hybridisation. Both are worth proper investment.
